free_text: Measurement of the 13C(alpha,n0)16O Differential
Cross Section from 0.8 to 6.5 MeV
#doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.132.062702
ODeSA
detectors.
Department of Physics, Gonzaga University,
Spokane, Washington 99258, USA
The University of Notre Dame Nuclear
Science Laboratory 5 MV Stable ion Accelerator for
Nuclear Astrophysics.
The Oak Ridge National Laboratory Deuterated
Spectroscopic Array (ODeSA). The array consisted of
nine deuterated scintillators mounted on a swing arm,
which was rotated to two different positions, with an
offset of 7.5 degree, in order to map 18 point
angular distributions between 0 and 157.5 degrees.
Enriched (99%) 13C powder was
evaporated onto a 0.5 mm Ta backing, which also
served as a beam stop, creating a thin layered
reference target of 10.3(6) microg/cm2. The target
thickness was determined by measuring the energy loss
over the narrow resonance in the 13C(alpha,n)16O
reaction at Ealpha = 1.05 MeV.
The uncertainties on the data points are
point-to-point, which are dominated by the unfolding
uncertainty and target degradation corrections. An
additional common mode systematic uncertainty of 13%
is also present that is not included.
Systematical uncertainty.
Charge collection.
Stopping power.
Intrinsic efficiency.
MCNP/Geometric efficiency.
The most significant corrections to the present data
came from neutron scattering on the target backing,
holder, and other beam components very close to the
target. These corrections were simulated using MCNP6,
where a detailed model of the setup was created.
These corrections were most significant at 0 and 90
degrees, up to 30%, but were less than 10%, otherwise.
